params.text:
The law behind EDI

The main piece of legislation in this area is the Equality Act 2010. It brought together many older laws into a single Act and makes it unlawful to discriminate against people because of certain aspects of who they are. These aspects are called protected characteristics, and there are nine of them.

The Equality Act works alongside the Human Rights Act 1998, which protects rights such as dignity, respect for private and family life, and freedom from degrading treatment. Regulators reinforce these duties: the Equality and Human Rights Commission (EHRC) enforces the Act, while the Care Quality Commission (CQC) expects providers to deliver care that is fair, person-centred and free from discrimination. Skills for Care supports the workforce to put these standards into everyday practice.

As a care worker you have both a legal duty and a professional duty to uphold equality – and your employer's policies and your Code of Conduct set out what that looks like in your role.

params.taskDescription: Drag the words into the gaps to complete these facts about equality legislation.
params.textField: The main law protecting people from discrimination is the *Equality* Act 2010. It identifies nine protected *characteristics*. The Act is enforced by the *EHRC*, while care services are inspected by the *CQC*. It works alongside the Human *Rights* Act 1998, which protects people's *dignity*.
